coredata.dc:title: Flexible, active P-typed copper(I) thiocyanate (p-CuSCN) films as self-powered photodetectors for large-scale optoelectronic systems
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Sancan
Zhao, Qingqiang
Hou, Qing
Ding, Yuanpeng
Quan, Jiale
Zhang, Yixin
Wu, Fangyu
Lu, Yifei
Zhang, Hehua
Li, Huijun
Wang, Ding
Song, Enming
coredata.dc:description: P-type copper(I) thiocyanate (p-CuSCN) semiconductor materials have attracted a great deal of attention in the application for microsystems and optoelectronic engineering. Major challenge is in the development of advanced fabrication/growth techniques and resultant high-efficiency devices. Herein, in situ grown p-CuSCN film with different morphology are successfully achieved on flexible Cu foil by the simple solid-liquid interface reaction, which displays excellent UV photoresponse due to effective charge transport, thereby contributing to the large-area fabrication technique and the high-performance operation. The self-powered, highly sensitive and flexible NGQDs/CuSCN heterojunction device shows the ultrahigh photoresponsivity of 1.6 A/W and detectivity of 0.8 × 1012 Jones at 3 V bias under 360 nm illumination, and the ultrafast photoresponse speed (Tr= 10 µs, Td=0.6 ms), with relatively stable performance under bending cycles. The results provides an easy-processing and promising route to fabricate large-area p-CuSCN with remarkable optoelectronic performance, which opens up a new avenue on more novel works for the material design in practical photodetection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: p-CuSCN
N-doped carbon dot
Photodetector
Self-powered
Flexible device

This work is supported by the STI 2030-Major Project No. 2022ZD0209900, National Natural Science Foundation of China (62071300, 62204057), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (21ZR1444200, 19ZR1435200, 20490761100, 22ZR1406400), Chenguang Scholar Project of Shanghai Education Commission (19CG52, 19CG53), Lingang Laboratory (Grant No.LG-QS-202202-02) and we appreciate the support by Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Major Project (Grant No. 2018SHZDZX01),the Open Fund of the State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics (IOSKL2020KF03), ZJ Lab, and Shanghai Center for Brain Science and Brain-Inspired Technology and the young scientist project of MOE innovation platform.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a Schematic illustration of the morphology transformation for CuSCN. The SEM images of b CuSCN(CH4O), c CuSCN(C2H6O), d CuSCN(H2O). e HRTEM image and the elemental mapping for CuSCN(CH4O) sample.
a the XRD pattern and UV-Vis spectrum b of CuSCN(CH4O), CuSCN(C2H6O) and CuSCN (H2O) samples. XPS spectra for copper c, carbon d, nitrogen e, and sulfur f regions for CuSCN(CH4O).

The crystal structure model of CuSCN a and NGQDs b. The partial charge densities of c CBM and d VBM states for NGQDs/CuSCN. e the charge density difference for NGQDs/CuSCN (electrons (purple) and holes (yellow)).

a Schematic diagram of the CuSCN-based photodetector. b I-V curves of CuSCN(CH4O), CuSCN(C2H6O) and CuSCN (H2O) samples at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm and the light power of 2.36 mW cm−2. c the comparison of on-off switch for CuSCN(CH4O), CuSCN(C2H6O) and CuSCN (H2O) samples. d Spectral responsivity curves of CuSCN(CH4O), CuSCN(C2H6O) and CuSCN (H2O) samples at a voltage of 3V.

a I-V and b I-T curves of CuSCN(CH4O) and NGQDs/CuSCN samples at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm and the light power of 2.36 mW cm−2. c the comparison of on-off switch and d the normalized I–T curves for CuSCN(CH4O) and NGQDs/CuSCN samples. Pulse response curve for e CuSCN(CH4O) and f NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O).

a IV curve of NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) at 360 nm excitation wavelength under different light intensity. b IT curves of NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) at 360 nm excitation wavelength with a voltage of 3V under different light intensity. c the corresponding fitted curve between the light intensity and the photocurrent. d IT curves of NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) at 360 nm excitation wavelength with a voltage of 0V. e IT curve of the NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) at 360 nm excitation wavelength without voltage under different light intensity. f the corresponding fitted curve between the light intensity and the photocurrent.

a The images of the bending test of NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O). b I-V curve of NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) at different wavelength. c Responsivity and Detectivity curves of NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) with a voltage of 3V. d I-T curve of the NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) at 360 nm excitation wavelength with a voltage of 3V under different bending angle. e the corresponding on/off ratio and Responsivity under different bending angle. f I-T curve of the NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) at 360nm with a voltage of 3V under different bending cycles. g the corresponding on/off ratio and responsivity under different bending cycles.

head.ce:title: Flexible, active P-typed copper(I) thiocyanate (p-CuSCN) films as self-powered photodetectors for large-scale optoelectronic systems

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

University of Shanghai for Science & Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shanghai Frontiers Science Research Base of Intelligent Optoelectronics and Perception, Institute of Optoelectronics, Fudan University, 2005 Songhu Rd, Shanghai, 200433, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Frontiers Science Research Base of Intelligent Optoelectronics and Perception

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: P-type copper(I) thiocyanate (p-CuSCN) semiconductor materials have attracted a great deal of attention in the application for microsystems and optoelectronic engineering. Major challenge is in the development of advanced fabrication/growth techniques and resultant high-efficiency devices. Herein, in situ grown p-CuSCN film with different morphology are successfully achieved on flexible Cu foil by the simple solid-liquid interface reaction, which displays excellent UV photoresponse due to effective charge transport, thereby contributing to the large-area fabrication technique and the high-performance operation. The self-powered, highly sensitive and flexible NGQDs/CuSCN heterojunction device shows the ultrahigh photoresponsivity of 1.6 A/W and detectivity of 0.8 × 1012 Jones at 3 V bias under 360 nm illumination, and the ultrafast photoresponse speed (Tr= 10 µs, Td=0.6 ms), with relatively stable performance under bending cycles. The results provides an easy-processing and promising route to fabricate large-area p-CuSCN with remarkable optoelectronic performance, which opens up a new avenue on more novel works for the material design in practical photodetection.

p-CuSCN film with different morphology are easily fabricated on flexible Cu foil via in situ transformation method, which could achieve high uniformity at the macroscopic scale and the intimate interface between p-CuSCN and the substrate. Furthermore, flexible and self-powered NGQDs/CuSCN heterojunction photodetector was constructed, which displayed the ultrahigh photoresponsivity, outperforming those of most reported p-type semiconductor PD.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The wearable optoelectronic devices and systems with good portable and flexibility that can be comfortably fitted with human clothes and body hold great promise for the applications in health monitoring, image sensing and various communication [1–5]. Such technologies of flexible photodetector serve as an important component in developing the next-generation optoelectronic system during the past few years. However, the great challenge is in the development of the design of flexible photodetector with both high efficiency and favorable mechanical strength. Suitable choices of novel materials with complex topography, design architectures and integration fashion offer opportunities to approach those of conventional wafer-based technologies, in some ways that establish flexible platforms to overcome such limitations [6–15].

Emerging classes of semiconductor materials of p-CuSCN with the band width of 3.6–3.9 eV and the hole mobility of ∼0.1 cm2v−1s−1, owns good chemical stability and easy processability [16–18], which makes it as one of the ideal hole transport layers in the light emitting diodes and solar cell during the past few decades [19–24]. Some of the most notable recent reported system of p-CuSCN photodetector at variable scales ranging from nanometer level to macroscopic areas, are of potential interest. For example, Lin et al. employed electrodeposited CuSCN film to achieve high efficiency deep-ultraviolet photodetector [25]. Anthopoulos et al. reported spin-coating CuSCN-based photodetector [18] exhibited a responsivity of 79 A/W at 280 nm at 220 μW cm−2. However, the daunting challenges remain in the combination of advanced fabrication/growth techniques and resultant high-performance operation. In all cases, the preparation strategies of CuSCN film mainly include electrochemical deposition [26], spray method [27], spin coating, drop casting and inkjet printing [25,27–29], These traditional manufacturing strategies are complex, and some of the preparation methods require the use of special equipment and materials, which increases costs and is incompatible with the complex morphology and topography of p-CuSCN films, further limiting the charge transfer of devices, which put a significant impediment in the flexible CuSCN-based photodetector devices.

Effective efforts to the achievement of flexible CuSCN-based photodetector devices are of particular interest in the advanced platforms with sophisticated modes of electronic materials and mechanics, where the key persistent features are compatible materials and mechanically compliant architectures. Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have attracted much attention due to their excellent chemical, optical properties and unique up-conversion ability, which is beneficial to the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [30–37]. The practical application of GQDs for the construction of multifunctional devices can be effectively modified by the doping of various elements (e.g. sulfur, nitrogen), or functionalizd with organic chains such as branched polyethylenimine(PEI), ethylenediamine (EDA). Xu et al. reported the high-output triboelectric nanogenerators by incorporating the PEI functionalized N-doped carbon dots (NGQDs) into the polyvinyl alcohol [38]. Biswas et al. demonstrated the formation of the heterojunction integration between NGQDs and CsPbBr3, evidencing the effective charge separation [39]. Those recent advances has established classes of NGQDs to advantages in rich surface chemical properties and good electrical conductivity and large specific surface area. Nevertheless, the study about the electron transfer between the interface engineering of NGQDs based composites has not been systematically reported, and the insightful mechanism still requires more investigation.

In the work, we report a simple interface reaction to fabricate CuSCN in different solvents (water, methanol and ethanol) via in situ grown on Cu foil, which are defined as CuSCN(CH4O), CuSCN(C2H6O) and CuSCN(H2O), respectively. The method is easy to implement, more importantly, the intimate interface between CuSCN and the substrate is achieved via in situ grown route. The constructed CuSCN-based photodetector displays excellent UV photoresponse. Furthermore, the comprehensive photodetection performance would be improved by the introduction of NGQDs. A strong interface interaction between CuSCN and NGQDs was demonstrated by Density functional theory (DFT) calculations, which evidenced the effective electron transfer from CuSCN to NGQDs across the interface. The NGQDs/CuSCN heterojunction device displays the ultrahigh photoresponsivity of 1.6 A/W and detectivity of 0.8 × 1012 Jones at 3 V bias under 360 nm illumination, and the ultrafast photoresponse speed (Tr=10 µs, Td=0.6 ms), outperforming the most reported p-type semiconductor-based photodetectors. Moreover, self-powered performance and remarkable flexibility of the devices are demonstrated. The flexibility of CuSCN-based high-performance photodetector provides high possibility for the development of further design in wearable and portable electronics.

The Cu foil was first pretreated by alkaline solution for 1 h, calcinated at 500 °C in an N2 atmosphere. As shown in Fig. S1, the diffraction peaks of the samples are indexed to the Cu2O (JCPDS card No. 34-1354). The peaks at 36.3 o, 42.3 o, 61.2 o and 74.3 o can be ascribed to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystal planes of Cu2O, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 43.3 o and 50.6 ° can be ascribed to (111) and (200) of Cu (JCPDS card No.04-0836). Fig. S2 demonstrated that the highly uniform Cu2O film has been obtained. The morphological transformation from the Cu2O foil to CuSCN with different solvents (methanol, ethanol, water) is depicted in Fig. 1 a, three morphologies CuSCN were obtained by simply adjusting growth solvent. As shown in Fig. 1b, CuSCN(CH4O) presents a spatial network structure with the hole size of ∼ 0.5 µm, it creates a large surface area and interconnected pathways, leading to improved contact between the active layer and the electrode. More specifically, the spatial network structure of the material provides a path for the photogenerated charge carriers to travel from the absorbing layer to the electrodes, which is beneficial to the effective electron transfer. Fig. 1c presents CuSCN(C2H6O) has cluster-like morphology with larger connected gaps compared with CuSCN(CH4O). The stacked particle-like CuSCN (H2O) is achieved when H2O has been used as growth solvent in Fig. 1d. In situ formation of CuSCN can be explained by the following reaction:
(1)
K S C N + a l c o h o l
(
H 2
O
)
→ H S C N
(2)
H S C N + C
u 2
O → C u S C N +
H 2
O

KSCN has been transformed to HSCN by alcoholysis or hydrolysis reaction, and then the interface reaction between HSCN and Cu2O would happen, resulting in the formation of CuSCN [40]. During in-situ interface growth from Cu2O to CuSCN, the nuclear molecules of CuSCN tend to bond with each other, in which these atomic groups are connected with each other, leaving only some isolated holes and channels, which results in the formation of the spatial network structure. As the solubility of CuSCN in different solvent varies a lot, the crystallization rate of CuSCN is different, leading to the formation of different morphology of CuSCN. In Fig. 1e, the high-resolution TEM image displays the interplanar lattice spacing of 3.3 and 2.59 Å, which corresponds to the (012) and (018) planes of CuSCN. The homogeneous dispersion of Cu, N, S, C elements of elemental mapping images in the selected a further evidenced the successful fabrication of CuSCN.

The corresponding XRD pattern for the CuSCN(CH4O), CuSCN(C2H6O) and CuSCN (H2O) samples are displayed in Fig. 2 a. It is noticed that despite of varied morphology of the synthesized CuSCN samples, all the diffraction peaks of the samples are indexed to the 3R-CuSCN (JCPDS card No. 73-1855). The peaks at 16.4o, 27.4o, 34.8o, 47.4o and 50.3o can be well indexed to the (006), (012), (018), (110) and (116) crystal planes of CuSCN, respectively. The sharp and strong diffraction peaks demonstrated that the well crystallized CuSCN can be obtained. Fig. 2b displays the UV-Vis spectra of CuSCN with different morphology, which have a negligible response toward visible light but a very strong absorption upon ultraviolet irradiation. In Fig. S3-S5, the band gaps of CuSCN with different morphology are calculated to be 3.70 eV CuSCN(CH4O), 3.70 eV CuSCN(C2H6O), and 3.71 eV CuSCN(H2O) separately. XPS study is characterized to further verify the elemental composition of CuSCN in Fig. 2c-f. The peaks of Cu 2p 3/2 and 2p1/2 located at the binding energies of 932.4 and 952.2 eV are the typical peaks for Cu+ [41,42]. The presence of other elements in CuSCN is verified by Fig. 2d-f and c shows the peak of C 1s at 285.6 eV, which corresponds to the sp-state carbon in the -SCN. The fitted N1s region is dominated by a peak at 398.3 eV, which can be ascribed to the nitrogen atom in the -SCN in Fig. 2(d). The S 2p spectrum has been fitted by sulfur 2p 2/3 and 2p 1/2, which is dominated by the peaks at 163.0 eV and 164.2 eV, which correspond to the sulfur in -SCN in Fig. 2e. The above results all verify the formation of CuSCN by the simple solid-liquid interface reaction. Meanwhile, the spectrum of C 1s, N 1s, S 2p all have small shoulders, which corresponds to the -CSN. To evidence the presence of NGQDs in NGQDs/CuSCN composites, Raman spectroscopy was carried out under an excitation wavelength of 532 nm in Fig. S6, two distinctive peaks at ≈1330 and ≈1550 cm−1 can be ascribed to D band and G band of graphene dot, G band is ascribed to the characteristic peak as primary in-plane vibration mode, and D band as the disorder vibration peak is used to characterize its defect which evidenced the existence of NGQDs in the composites [43]. In Fig. S7, The TEM map of NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) is displayed. It can be clearly seen that the surface of CH4O is covered with a large number of spherical NGQDs, which are evenly distributed and the size is between 2 and 10 nm. Fig. S8 evidenced higher light absorption than that of pure CuSCN, which is benefical to the photodetection performance.

To further understand the mechanism of interaction for NGQDs/CuSCN heterojunction, the density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out to investigate the interface electronic structures. The crystal structures of CuSCN and NGQDs are displayed in Fig. 3 a and b. Furthermore, Fig. 3c and d show the charge density of Conduction band minimum (CBM) and Valence band maximum (VBM) states of NGQDs/CuSCN heterojunction. The spatial separation of hole-rich region on CuSCN and electron-rich region on NGQDs was observed after the decoration NGQDs on the CuSCN film, the redistributed charge carriers in the NGQDs/CuSCN interface could effectively decrease the recombination of charge carriers. Furthermore, the charge density difference was conducted to analyze the electron property in Fig. 3e, which evidenced the effective electron transfer from CuSCN to NGQDs across the interface. The observation indicates strong interface interaction happens on the NGQDs/CuSCN interface, which is beneficial to efficient charge separation, resulting in excellent photodetection.

The optoelectronic characteristics of the as-grown CuSCN nanostructure in different solvents was conducted by manually patterning silver (Ag) contacts at the surface of film, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 4 a. Fig. 4b compared the current–voltage (I–V) characteristic at the voltage from -3 to 3 V for the CuSCN with different morphology photodetector, which displays a relative symmetric dark-current and photocurrent under the illumination from 340 nm, however, all the CuSCN with different morphology photodetectors display a slight rectifying character, in which CuSCN (CH4O), CuSCN (C2H5OH), CuSCN (H2O) photodetectors yields dark current of 1.2 × 10−7 A, 7.9 × 10−8 A and 1.1 × 10−8 A at 3 V, respectively. Meanwhile, the dark current of CuSCN (H2O) is obviously lower than that of CuSCN synthesized in alcohols, which may be attributed to high concentration defects in CuSCN(H2O). The on-off switch in one cycle was shown in Fig. 4c, the rise and fall times for the detectors are all within several seconds. Responsivity is an important parameter to define the sensitivity of the photodetector. The responsivity spectrum for the CuSCN photodetector at a bias of 3V ranging from ∼ 300 nm to ∼ 500 nm is shown in Fig. 4d. An obvious high responsivity can be observed over the violet region, the curve trend is consistent with UV-vis absorption spectrum. The dominant peaks at the wavelength of 340 nm can reach to 3.7 mA/W CuSCN (CH4O), 1.8 mA/W CuSCN (C2H5OH) and 0.4 mA/W CuSCN (H2O). The best photo-responsivity characteristics for CuSCN (CH4O) can be ascribed to the spatial network structure, which is effective to carrier transfer.

NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) and CuSCN (CH4O) photodetectors were constructed by two-probe method with silver electrode. Fig. 5 a shows a typical I−V curve for the devices under dark and 360 nm light illumination. CuSCN (CH4O) device yields the dark current of 1.2 × 10−7 A at 3 V, however, the dark current for NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) photodetector decreases to 4.2 × 10−11 A, which is nearly three orders of magnitudes lower than that of CuSCN (CH4O) device. The phenomenon can be explained by the reducing conductivity due to the existence of the NGQDs. The I–T curve under a bias of 3 V is presented in Fig. 5b, the photocurrent changed sensitively and remained stable under several mechanical chopping turning on and off, which verify excellent repeatability of the device. Fig. 5c shows that the photocurrent of CuSCN (CH4O) and NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) device increased to a stable value at ∼ 9.6 × 10−6 A and 1.9 × 10−8 quickly when the light is on, the on-off ratio of CuSCN (CH4O) and NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) device increases from 68 to 387, which is resulted from the increased photocurrent and decreased dark current, suggesting that NGQDs plays an important role in the photosensitivity of the device. Moreover, the normalized I–T curves in one cycle for CuSCN (CH4O) and NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) device were displayed in Fig. 5d, the rise and fall time of NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) device are both faster than that of CuSCN(CH4O). Furthermore, the femtosecond pulsed laser was applied to evaluate the rise and decay time, as displayed in Fig. 5e and d. The rise and decay time are calculated as 1 and 8 ms for CuSCN(CH4O) photodetector, 10 µs and 0.6 ms for NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) photodetector, which shows fast response speed for NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) photodetector, The reason is that heterojunction improves carrier mobility and promotes the fast and efficient movement of multiple carriers, evidencing the effective electron transfer [44]. The result is consistent with the observation of DFT calculation.

Fig. 6 a displays the I-V curves for NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) device under different light intensity, the photocurrent increases with the increasing light intensity at the wavelength of 360 nm. It exhibits slight rectifying characteristics. The rectification ratio of RR (±3V) is 0.8. The photocurrent values are 3.9 × 10−9 A at 0.45 mW cm−2, 5.9 × 10−9 A at 0.95 mW cm−2, 1.0 × 10−8 A at 1.43 mW cm−2, 1.5 × 10−8 A at 1.89 mW cm−2 and 1.9 × 10−8 A at 2.36 mW cm−2 in Fig. 6b, evidencing the generation efficiency of the photocarrier is proportional to the absorbed photon. The linear relationship between the photocurrent and illuminance intensity is fitted by the following equation:
(3)
I
p h
= A
P
θ
in which A is a constant, and θ is the exponent to demonstrate the relationship between the photocurrent to light intensity. θ value of 0.97 is observed in Fig. 6c, which indicates high-quality interface with few electron-hole traps. The on/off ratio of 15 is shown in I–T curves under dark and light illumination of 360 nm at 0 V, which evidenced the self-powered behavior for NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) device in Fig. 6d. The self-powered behavior of NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) is attributed to its slight rectification characteristics [45]. Meanwhile, the photocurrent for NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) device at 0 V is increasing with the increased light intensity, and nonlinear behavior for θ value of 0.89 is observed in Fig. 6e and f, suggesting a relatively complex carrier transfer process under 0 V bias.

The NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) photodetector constructed by Cr/Au electrodes with the channel length of 1 mm was systematically investigated the flexible features. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the flexible device with the bending angles from 0°, 30°,45°, 60°, 90° was charactered. The I-V curves in Fig. 7b for the device show a dark current of 8.5 × 10−11 A at the bias of 3 V, and yields the photocurrent of 3.3 × 10−8, 2.4 × 10−8, 1.4 × 10−8, 7.5 × 10−9 A at 360, 400, 300 and 460 nm illumination at 3 V bias. In Fig. 7c, the trend of Rλ and D* curves is consistent with that of UV-Vis spectrum, indicating the effective light absorption, and the maximum Rλ and D* can reach 1.6 A/W and 0.8 × 1012 Jones. As can be seen in Fig. 7d and e, the flexible device displays declined tendency of the dark current with the increasing of bending angle, because the formation of the tiny cracks could result in the improved resistance during the bending test. In addition, the repeated bending tests were performed to explore the variation of photodetecting property in Fig. 7f and 7g. The stable on/off ratio was displayed after repeating 600 bending cycles, A sudden decrease in on/off ratio was displayed until the bending cycles is above 700. All above results indicate the well adhesion and intimate contact between CuSCN and the substrate, which is beneficial to the effective carrier transfer. The summaries of performance of some p-type photodetector were displayed in Table S1, which outperformed the most reported p-type photodetector. The stable and superior flexible property of the NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) photodetector under bending test indicates its great potentials in wearable electronic device.

In conclusion, we demonstrate the fabrication of large-area and novel CuSCN film with different morphology, the high on/off ratio for CuSCN (CH4O), CuSCN (C2H5OH), CuSCN (H2O) photodetectors can achieve to 80, 55 and 90. The design of flexible NGQDs/CuSCN heterojunction photodetector with superhigh photodetection performance was demonstrated. The photoresponsivity and detectivity is as high as 1.6 A/W and 0.8 × 1012 Jones at 3 V bias under 360 nm illumination, and the ultrafast photoresponse speed with Tr= 10 µs and Td=0.6 ms is demonstrated. The NGQDs/CuSCN heterojunction film UV PD can also operate without external power supply. The excellent photoelectric properties of NGQDs/CuSCN heterojunction provide a new scheme for the application of the microstructure UV detector.

Cu foil (99.99%, 1 cm × 1 cm) were cleaned with diluted hydrochloric acid (aq, 0.1 mol/L), ethanol, and deionized water successively in ultrasonic bath and then dried with a N2 gun. the Cu foils were immersed in the solution of 13.5 ml NaOH (aq, 1 mol/L), 6.5 ml (NH4)2SO4 (aq, 0.1 mol/L), deionized water (30 mL) under constant stirring. After reacting at room temperature for 1 h, the obtained sample was further calcinated at 500°C for 1 h in an N2 atmosphere to obtain Cu2O nanofilm. A constant flow of N2 (99.99%) with 80 standard cubic centimeters per minute (sccm) was used as the shielding gas throughout the process. The as-prepared Cu2O nanofilm was added into KSCN (aq, 5mmol/L) solution with different solvents (water, methanol and ethanol), which are named as CuSCN(H2O), CuSCN(CH4O), CuSCN(C2H6O), respectively.

1.68 g citric acid and 1.44 g urea dissolve together in 40 ml of deionized water. After stirring for 5 min, the above mixture solution was transferred into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Consequently, the autoclave was heat at 180 ℃ for 8 h. The as-synthesized product was collected and filtrated with ethanol and then dried overnight at 80 ℃, which is named as NGQDs. 5mg NGQDs was dissolved into 50 ml of 5mmol/L KSCN solution, then the CuSCN film was put in the solution and reacted for 1d, and dried in an oven at 80 ℃ for 30 min.

The CuSCN and NGQDs/CuSCN photodetectors were assembled by two-probe method with silver electrode. Furthermore, the NGQDs/CuSCN(CH4O) photodetector constructed by Cr/Au electrodes with the channel length of 1 mm was systematically investigated the flexible features, the geometric shape of the device is a flat plate, the size of the device is 1 cm2, and the size and spacing of the silver electrode of the device are about 0.1mm and 1mm respectively, lighting area is 0.1mm2. The light intensity was measured with a NOVA II power meter (OPHIR photonics). The response time of photodetector is studied by femtosecond laser and a digital oscilloscope (Tektronix DPO 5140B). The photoelectric properties of the PDs were recorded using the semiconductor characterization system (Keithley 4200-SCS), connected to a 75 W Xenon arc lamp with a monochromator. The light intensity was determined with a NOVA II power meter (OPHIR Photonics). All the measurements were performed at room temperature. The detectivity (D*) and spectral responsivity (Rλ) to evaluate the photoelectronic property are defined according to the following equation:
(4)
R λ
=
I
P h
−
I d
P S
(5)
D
*
=
R λ
( 2 e
I d
/ S )
1 / 2
where Id and Iph are the dark current and photocurrent. e, P and S refer to the electronic charge, light power density, the effective area under irradiation.

The morphology of CuSCN film was traced by field emission scanning electron microscope (FEI Quanta 450FEG) and TEM (TECNAI F30). The phase was identified by XRD (Bruker D8-A25) with CuK radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). Raman spectroscopy was performed on a spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution) with 532 nm laser excitation. XPS was conducted by a spectrometer (ESCALAB 250 X-ray).

All calculations in this study were performed with the Vienna ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) within the frame of density functional theory (DFT) [46]. The exchange-correlation interactions of electron were described via the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with PBE functional [47], and the projector augmented wave (PAW) method [48] was used to describe the interactions of electron and ion. Additionally, the DFT-D3 method [49,50] was used to account for the long-range van der Waals forces present within the system. The Monkhorst-Pack scheme [51] with a 2×2×1 k-point mesh was used for the integration in the irreducible Brillouin zone. The kinetic energy cut-off of 500 eV was chosen for the plane wave expansion. The lattice parameters and ionic position were fully relaxed, and the total energy was converged within 10-5 eV per formula unit. The final forces on all ions are less than 0.02/Å.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S.C. Han, Q.Q. Zhao and Q. Hou contributed equally to this work. This work is supported by the STI 2030-Major Project No. 2022ZD0209900, National Natural Science Foundation of China (62071300, 62204057), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (21ZR1444200, 19ZR1435200, 20490761100, 22ZR1406400), Chenguang Scholar Project of Shanghai Education Commission (19CG52, 19CG53), Lingang Laboratory (Grant No.LG-QS-202202-02) and we appreciate the support by Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Major Project (Grant No. 2018SHZDZX01),the Open Fund of the State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics (IOSKL2020KF03), ZJ Lab, and Shanghai Center for Brain Science and Brain-Inspired Technology and the young scientist project of MOE innovation platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.mtelec.2023.100048.
